articles.headline: Constitution and By-Laws of the Philadelphia Association for the Moral and Mental Improvement of the People of Color
articles.summary: Full reprint of the constitution of a Philadelphia benevolent association devoted to encouraging agricultural pursuits (including settlement in Upper Canada) and to clothing poor children so they can attend the Free Schools. Officers: President, Vice-President, Treasurer, Corresponding Secretary, Recording Secretary, Assistant Secretary, a nine-member Board of Managers, a five-member soliciting committee, and a seven-member superintending committee tasked with visiting every colored family in the city monthly. Note: the article is immediately followed on the page by an unrelated signature line reading 'Philip A. Bell, Proprietor' -- almost certainly a masthead/column-layout artifact rather than an actual signer of this Philadelphia document, since Bell was the New York paper's proprietor.

articles.headline: Letter from Richard Johnson, New Bedford agent, on subscriber drive
articles.author: Richard Johnson
articles.summary: New Bedford agent Richard Johnson writes (June 3, 1837) explaining that the money pressure/panic has depressed subscriptions but promises a coming meeting of New Bedford's colored people and expects subscriber numbers to quadruple; editorial reply praises the sentiment and invokes Garrison as Massachusetts's abolitionist pioneer.

articles.headline: 'A Colored Baltimorean' -- second letter criticizing the colored ministry
articles.author: A Colored Baltimorean (pseudonym)
articles.summary: Baltimore correspondent (June 17, 1837) continues a critique of under-qualified colored ministers, arguing the practice fosters spiritual pride among the uninformed and alienates the more intelligent and educated members of Black congregations; describes the humiliation of Black worshippers relegated to segregated seating in white-run churches even while being denied genuine 'fellowship.' Promises a future letter on his own denomination's nine-month effort to improve ministerial education.

articles.headline: Wm. Yates letter: formation of the 'Leavitt Anti-Slavery Society for People of Color' in Philadelphia
articles.author: Wm. Yates
articles.summary: Philadelphia correspondent Wm. Yates (June 19, 1837) reports that the annual council of the Philadelphia Association for Mental and Moral Improvement led to a large meeting -- addressed by 'Brother Burleigh' (C. C. Burleigh) -- which formed a new anti-slavery society named, unprompted, the 'Leavitt Anti-Slavery Society for People of Color' in tribute to Rev. Joshua Leavitt's labors among Philadelphia's Black congregations. Compares this to similarly named benevolent societies in Albany: the Lundy Benevolent Society, the Tappan [Benevolent Society], and the Garrison Benevolent Society.

articles.headline: Charles B. Ray departs as Traveling Agent for New England
articles.author: Editor and Proprietor
articles.summary: Announces that Rev. Charles B. Ray will leave New York for New England as the paper's Traveling Agent, soliciting subscriptions and support; praised as a minister of intelligence and integrity.

articles.headline: New York Philomathean Society library committee appeal
articles.summary: The New York Philomathean Society (est. 1830), devoted to 'improvement of literature and useful knowledge,' solicits book and money donations to grow its library (then 500-600 volumes). Committee: Prs. R. Bastien (Librarian, 290 Bleecker St.), Edward V. Clark (Assistant Librarian, 527 Pearl St.), and P. A. Bell (Office of the Colored American).

articles.headline: Robert Hamilton appointed City Agent and Collector
articles.summary: Mr. Robert Hamilton is appointed City Agent and Collector for the Colored American; all business transacted by him acknowledged as lawful by the Proprietor.

articles.headline: Phoenix Society bequest dispute over a deceased benefactor's education fund
articles.summary: Recounts that a deceased white benefactor left several thousand dollars in trust to his old friend Israel Corse and to Arthur Tappan (his 'coadjutor'), to be applied toward the education and benefit of colored youth. Because Corse and Tappan disagreed over whether the money should support the New York Phoenix Society or a Manual Labor College Tappan and Simeon Jocelyn wished to establish, the funds remained tied up in the bank, benefiting neither; the Phoenix Society, meanwhile, had to give up a school for want of means.

articles.headline: Colored Methodist conference resolutions on ministerial education, temperance, dress, and gratitude to colleges
articles.author: Wm. Yates
articles.summary: A colored Methodist annual conference (transmitted via Wm. Yates) adopts resolutions: directing the Bishop to document obstacles colored ministerial candidates face in obtaining education and to petition Northern colleges/seminaries to admit students without distinction of color; urging education generally; endorsing total abstinence temperance; requiring punctual attendance at worship; and warning against both extravagant dress and ragged appearance. A closing resolution thanks Northern institutions that already admit students 'without distinction of color.'

articles.headline: New agent appointments: Cincinnati, Poughkeepsie, Lancaster PA circuit, Palmyra and Farmington NY
articles.summary: New paper agents announced: George Cary of Cincinnati, Ohio (on recommendation of 'our worthy friend Hugh[es]on'); N. Brown of Poughkeepsie, NY; Rev. Henry C. Turner of Philadelphia, for the Lancaster (Pa.) circuit of the Bethel Methodists; and Orson Carr of Palmyra, NY, and Asa B. Smith of Farmington, NY.

articles.headline: Rev. Theodore S. Wright's speech at the New York State Anti-Slavery Society anniversary
articles.author: Theodore S. Wright
articles.summary: At the recent anniversary of the New York State Anti-Slavery Society, Rev. Theodore S. Wright of New York speaks on a resolution condemning prejudice against free colored citizens in worship, travel (steamboats, stages), public houses, and schools as 'the spirit of slavery.' Speaking as a personally affected Black minister, Wright describes prejudice as an ever-present atmosphere even for free, respectable colored men, and states it leads some colored parents to wish their children had never been born.

articles.headline: Obituaries: Rev. Joseph [Sturge/Labe] of New Brunswick, and Rev. Wm. Levingston of Baltimore
articles.author: T.S.W.
articles.summary: Letter (New York, July 1837, signed T.S.W.) reports the deaths of two colored ministers: a minister of New Brunswick, NJ (name badly OCR-garbled, rendered 'Rev. Joseph Labe'), and Rev. Wm. Levingston, rector of the colored Episcopal Church of Baltimore, described as tireless in the cause of religion. Requests the Colored American formally note their deaths.

articles.headline: Obituary: Miss Amberdore Ellsworth
articles.summary: Miss Amberdore Ellsworth died June 24, 1837 after several months' illness (the final five weeks of severe suffering) from consumption; account describes visits from the author, who found her calm and spiritually prepared for death, comforting her weeping friends at her bedside.

articles.headline: New York City Committee of Vigilance monthly meeting notice; Dixon fugitive case
articles.author: David Ruggles
articles.summary: Notice of the regular monthly meeting of the New York City Committee of Vigilance, Broadway Hall, signed David Ruggles, Secretary. Same page reports that about $300 had to be raised, through church donations/collections and the Vigilance Committee, before a man named Dixon could be liberated (context suggests a fugitive or debt-bondage case); balance still being collected by pledge.

articles.headline: Salmon P. Chase's argument in the case of Matilda, Hamilton County (Ohio) Court of Common Pleas
articles.author: Salmon P. Chase
articles.summary: Reprints the speech of Salmon P. Chase (future U.S. Chief Justice, then a young Cincinnati lawyer) arguing before the Hamilton County, Ohio Court of Common Pleas on March 11, 1837, on a writ of habeas corpus in the case of a colored woman named Matilda, held as a fugitive slave. Chase and a Mr. S. Eells argued for Matilda's freedom; opposing counsel argued for the claimant. Chase frames the case's gravity: error on one side risks consigning a rightfully free person to bondage, on the other merely deprives a claimant of one enslaved person's services.

articles.headline: Young Men's Anti-Slavery Society circular: survey of formerly enslaved city residents
articles.summary: Circular reporting that the managers of the Young Men's Anti-Slavery Society appointed a committee to document the number of persons in the city formerly held in slavery, how and by whom they were emancipated (including any purchase amounts and purchasers), to rebut pro-slavery press claims that free Black people are idle and incapable. Committee: H. Dresser, Esq., H. W. Barney, Esq., [W.] P. Johnson, G. R. Barker, and Rev. Mr. Ray (Charles B. Ray); Barker to be reached at 604 Wall St.

articles.headline: Wilberforce Colony (Upper Canada) rebuked by the Baptist Home Mission Society
articles.summary: Reprint from the Upper Canada Baptist Missionary Magazine of resolutions by the Board of the Home Mission Society of Upper Canada (Feb. 16, 1837), criticizing the Wilberforce Colony (a Black settlement of about twenty families in western Upper Canada) for sending fundraising agents through the U.S. and Britain on 'enormous salaries' that consumed most donations while doing little for the colony. The Board, after sending a two-man deputation to inspect the colony, resolves the colony's fundraising practice is 'injudicious' if not an imposition, and warns the public against its agents absent outside credentials. Signed Wm. Rees, President, and George J. Ryan.

articles.headline: Obituary: Mrs. Margaret Lee of Belleville, New Jersey
articles.summary: Reprinted from the Christian Advocate and Journal: Mrs. Margaret Lee, age 44, wife of Brother John Lee (a local Methodist Episcopal preacher), died May 28, 1837 at Belleville, NJ. Her funeral the next day drew a large concourse; services were led at her home by Rev. John B. Swaine, continued at the Methodist church by Rev. Mr. Myers (of the Dutch Reformed Church) and Revs. Dow and Nicholson, and closed at the grave by Rev. Walter Burrows.

articles.headline: New agency: Captain E. J. Pompey of Nantucket
articles.summary: General Agent Charles B. Ray appoints Captain E. J. Pompey of Nantucket, Massachusetts as paper agent for that place.

events.name: Pittsburgh meeting of the people of color on the threatened loss of Pennsylvania suffrage
events.description: A meeting of Pittsburgh's people of color convened at Bethel Church to consider efforts underway at the Pennsylvania Constitutional Convention in Harrisburg to strip Black Pennsylvanians of the right of suffrage they had held for forty-nine years. The Colored American's account of the parallel Philadelphia Bethel meeting was read and approved. Resolutions: a fifteen-member committee to take a statistical account of Pittsburgh's Black community (churches, schools, taxes paid, freeholders); two delegates to represent Pittsburgh at Harrisburg if needed; Lewis Woodson and John B. Vashon appointed as delegates to carry Pittsburgh's representation.

events.name: Phoenixonian Literary Society anniversary exercises
events.description: Anniversary of the Phoenixonian Literary Society (young men aged 15 and up); addresses by Samuel R. Ward and Henry Highland Garnet praised for composition and delivery; a dialogue performed by Downing and Ennis; an essay 'Character' by Charles L. Reason; a poem 'The Captive's Dream of Liberty,' composed and recited by Henry Highland Garnet; and the Anniversary Oration, 'The Philosophy of the Fine Arts,' delivered by Patrick Reason.
attendees: address; recited own poem 'The Captive's Dream of Liberty'
Anniversary Oration, 'The Philosophy of the Fine Arts'

events.name: Young Men's Anti-Slavery Society committee to document formerly enslaved city residents
events.description: The managers of the Young Men's Anti-Slavery Society appointed a committee to collect facts on the number of city residents formerly held in slavery and how they were emancipated, intended to counter pro-slavery press claims about free Black incapacity.

events.name: United Anti-Slavery Society First Anniversary -- British West Indies Emancipation celebration
events.description: The United Anti-Slavery Society of New York City announces its First Anniversary celebration on August 1, 1837, commemorating the August 1, 1834 emancipation of 800,000 people in the British West Indies; an ode to be sung by the choir and addresses by several gentlemen.

events.name: Founding of the Leavitt Anti-Slavery Society for People of Color
events.description: Following the annual council of the Philadelphia Association for Mental and Moral Improvement, a large meeting -- addressed by C. C. Burleigh -- voted to form a general anti-slavery society; the drafted constitution, left blank for a name, came back titled 'Leavitt Anti-Slavery Society for People of Color' in tribute to Rev. Joshua Leavitt's work among Philadelphia's Black congregations during the recent General Assembly meeting.
attendees: honoree; had lectured to the Second Presbyterian Congregation of Colored People
